title: Understand: Introduction to the Separation of Powers
description: Check your understanding of the separation of powers with this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: Introduction to the Separation of Powers
Understand: Introduction to the Separation of Powers

assessment_items.question_content:
**In the founders’ view, the best way to protect the unalienable rights promised by the Declaration of Independence was \_\_\_\_.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: to create a constitution that required the separation of powers
to elect a virtuous president like George Washington
to set up a very weak central government

assessment_items.question_content:
**The term “separation of powers” refers to the fact that \_\_\_\_.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the Constitution distributes power among three branches of government
the American colonies had recently separated from the British government
the smaller states wanted as much power as the larger states
the new national government could take away certain powers

assessment_items.question_content:
**What three branches of government are outlined in the Constitution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**The Constitution established a system of checks and balances that grants \_\_\_\_.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: only Congress the power to check other branches.
only the president the power to check other branches.
only the Supreme Court the ability to check other branches
each branch the power to check abuses by the other branches.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is true about the president’s veto power, as established by the Constitution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The president can veto laws passed by Congress.
The president’s veto can never be overruled.
Congress can override a president’s veto.
The president can veto a law passed by a state.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which part of the federal government has the power to hear legal cases and strike down laws which are unconstitutional?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Which of these functions of government is an example of checks and balances?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Which section of the U. S. Constitution describes the powers of the legislative branch?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Which part of the federal government nominates judges to fill a vacancies on the Supreme Court?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Which part of the government is responsible for confirming nominees for ambassador positions?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Which part of the government can strike down an executive order as unconstitutional?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Which part of the government is responsible for deciding whether to impeach a federal officer?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
